title: Soft pretzels — baking soda bath
subtitle: German-style soft pretzels with a dark chewy crust
excerpt: Yeasted dough shaped into the classic knot, briefly boiled in a baking soda solution to create the characteristic brown, chewy crust, then baked and finished with coarse salt. The baking soda bath is the home-baker's alternative to the lye bath used professionally.
sourceNotes: Soft pretzels (Laugenbrezel) are a Central European baking tradition. The alkaline bath that creates the crust is historically a lye solution; the baking soda alternative used here produces a similar Maillard reaction at a safer pH for home baking. The basic formula of flour, water, salt, yeast, and an alkaline pre-treatment is consistent across modern German home baking guides. The shaping technique (the two-handed cross-and-press) is the standard method taught in German baking apprenticeships.

content.text: is what makes a pretzel a pretzel. Without it, you have shaped bread. The 30-second boil raises the surface pH and gives the oven the alkaline conditions it needs to produce the deep mahogany colour and chewy exterior that are impossible to achieve by baking alone. The dough itself is straightforward: a simple, low-hydration wheat dough without enrichment. The shaping takes a little practice but is not difficult.

Combine flour, yeast, sugar, and salt in a large bowl (keep yeast and salt apart initially). Add the warm water and melted butter. Mix to a firm dough and knead for 8 minutes by hand until smooth. The dough should be stiff, not sticky. Shape into a ball, cover, and leave to rise for 60 minutes until noticeably puffed.

Divide the dough into 8 equal pieces (about 110 g each). Roll each piece into a rope about 55 cm long, slightly thicker in the centre and tapered at the ends. To shape: bring the ends up and cross them twice to make a loose loop, then fold the crossed ends down onto the lower arc of the loop and press to secure. Place on a lined baking tray. Leave to rest for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan). Bring 1 litre of water to a boil in a wide saucepan and add the bicarbonate of soda (it will foam up, so add it gradually). Lower the pretzels into the bath one at a time and boil for 30 seconds. Lift out with a slotted spoon, return to the lined tray, and immediately sprinkle with coarse salt flakes. Bake for 15 to 18 minutes until deep brown and set. Cool on a rack for at least 10 minutes.
